title: matteo-24
text: Hi, I am Matteo Glaizal. I am a French business student at Montpellier Business School, currently completing an Erasmus exchange at DCU in Dublin. I am driven by curiosity, adaptability, and a strong commitment to excellence. Welcome to my ePortfolio, where I showcase my academic journey and data management projects

title: About
text: I am a French Business Administration student at Montpellier Business School (MBS), currently expanding my international perspective through an Erasmus semester at Dublin City University (DCU). Ranked 8th out of a cohort of 471 students, I am deeply committed to academic excellence and continuous personal growth.My professional background spans from customer relationship management in the banking sector (Crédit Agricole) to an upcoming role in real estate, which has helped me develop strong adaptability and client-facing skills. I thrive in dynamic, international environments and constantly seek out new challenges—whether it is running a half-marathon, joining the finance society, or taking up volleyball and archery in Dublin.Objective: I am currently seeking a 12-month work-study program (alternance) in Business Development and Client Relations starting in September 2026.

title: Module rerview
text:
Module Reflection Throughout this Workflow & Data Management module, I have significantly expanded my technical skill set. Coming from a pure business background, diving into coding and web development was a rewarding challenge.
I discovered a lot of things; Firstly I learned how to build and structure a professional website using Quarto and successfully deployed it using GitHub Pages. Mastering version control with Git taught me the crucial importance of organized workflows and proper repository management. Furthermore, learning to integrate executable Python code into my projects bridged the gap between raw data analysis and clear, professional presentation. I learned as well to use Powershell, VS code and other new software for me.
This module not only improved my technical agility but also proved that a “growth mindset” is essential for adapting to today’s data-driven business world.
The course format, with learning by doing exercises, was perfect for me because it allowed me to do concrete things and feel like I was progressing with each session.

title: The Road to 21.1km: A Data-Driven Training Journey
text: In September, I set myself a goal: to run a half-marathon by the end of April. As someone new to long-distance running, I wanted to document my progress and see how my training volume directly impacted my performance in intermediate races.

title: The Road to 21.1km: A Data-Driven Training Journey
text:
Training Volume Evolution
The following interactive chart shows my weekly training volume (in hours) from the start of my journey in September to the race month in April.


Code
import plotly.express as px
import pandas as pd

# Creating the dataset based on my training logs
data = {
    'Month': ['Sept', 'Oct', 'Nov', 'Dec', 'Jan', 'Feb', 'Mar', 'Apr'],
    'Hours per Week': [1, 1, 2, 3, 4, 4, 0, 7],
    'Phase': ['Beginning', 'Baseline', '5km Prep', 'Building', '10km Prep', 'Peak', 'Slump', 'Final Push']
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Creating an interactive bar chart with Plotly
fig = px.bar(df, x='Month', y='Hours per Week', 
             title='Weekly Training Volume (Sept - Apr)',
             text='Hours per Week',
             hover_data=['Phase'],
             color='Hours per Week',
             color_continuous_scale='Viridis')

fig.update_traces(texttemplate='%{text}h', textposition='outside')
fig.update_layout(yaxis_title="Volume (Hours/Week)", xaxis_title="Timeline")

fig.show()


                            
                                            


Analysis of the Slump As shown in the chart, March was a difficult month. Between a heavy workload at university and a temporary loss of motivation, my training volume dropped to zero.
However, realizing the deadline was approaching, I managed a “Final Push” in April, peaking at 7 hours of training per week. This recovery was crucial to build the necessary endurance for the 21.1km distance.
Milestones & Results Throughout this journey, I participated in two intermediate races to test my fitness:
November: Completed a 5km in 28 minutes.
January: Completed a 10km in 57 minutes.
Final Performance: The Half-Marathon On April 15th, I finally crossed the finish line of my first half-marathon with a time of 1:53. Despite the inconsistent training in March, the high-intensity preparation in April allowed me to maintain a steady pace and exceed all my limits
Conclusion Using Python to visualize my training helped me stay accountable. Seeing the “zero” in March visually represented on the chart was a wake-up call that motivated the final success of this project.

title: Beyond the Horizon: My Erasmus Travel Diary
text:
I grew up in Montpellier, a sunny city in the South of France. For a long time, my world was relatively small; I had never really traveled or explored beyond my comfort zone. When I was accepted for an Erasmus year in Dublin, I knew it would be my “Challenge Number 1”.
Dublin didn’t just become my new home; it became my launchpad.

title: Beyond the Horizon: My Erasmus Travel Diary
text:
I grew up in Montpellier, a sunny city in the South of France. For a long time, my world was relatively small; I had never really traveled or explored beyond my comfort zone. When I was accepted for an Erasmus year in Dublin, I knew it would be my “Challenge Number 1”.
Dublin didn’t just become my new home; it became my launchpad.

title: Beyond the Horizon: My Erasmus Travel Diary
text:
Mapping the Journey
The map below visualizes my progression. Each point represents a barrier broken, from my first night away in Galway to solo adventures in the Highlands and cultural immersions in Africa.


Code
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import pandas as pd

# Data for my travels
travels = pd.DataFrame({
    'City': ['Montpellier', 'Dublin', 'Galway', 'Edinburgh', 'Morocco', 'Sweden'],
    'Lat': [43.61, 53.34, 53.27, 55.95, 31.62, 59.32],
    'Lon': [3.87, -6.26, -9.05, -3.18, -7.98, 18.06],
    'Note': [
        'Where it all started', 
        'My Erasmus Base (Challenge #1)', 
        'First step: A short night away', 
        'First solo trip: Independence', 
        'Cultural discovery', 
        'Connecting with nature'
    ]
})

fig = go.Figure()

# Add the points
fig.add_trace(go.Scattergeo(
    locationmode = 'ISO-3',
    lat = travels['Lat'],
    lon = travels['Lon'],
    hoverinfo = 'text',
    text = travels['City'] + ": " + travels['Note'],
    mode = 'markers+text',
    marker = dict(
        size = 12,
        color = '#e74c3c',
        line = dict(width = 2, color = 'white')
    ),
    textposition=["top center", "middle right", "middle left", "top center", "bottom center", "top right"]
))

# Configure the map layout
fig.update_layout(
    title = 'Erasmus Travels from Dublin',
    geo = dict(
        scope = 'world',
        projection_type = 'azimuthal equal area',
        showland = True,
        landcolor = "rgb(243, 243, 243)",
        countrycolor = "rgb(204, 204, 204)",
        lataxis = dict(range=[30, 70]),
        lonaxis = dict(range=[-20, 30]),
    ),
    margin={"r":0,"t":40,"l":0,"b":0}
)

fig.show()


                            
                                            


The Lessons Learned Each destination taught me something different about the world and myself:
Galway (The First Step): A simple overnight trip that proved I could manage logistics on my own in a foreign country.
Edinburgh (The Solo Leap): My first time traveling completely alone. Walking through the Scottish streets by myself was a massive boost in confidence.
Morocco (The Cultural Shift): My first time outside of Europe. It was an eye-opening experience that taught me to appreciate and navigate different traditions and environments.
Sweden (The Wild): A week dedicated to nature. It taught me the importance of slowing down and reconnecting with the environment.
Conclusion A year ago, I was a student who had never left home. Today, thanks to this Erasmus experience, I see the world as a map of opportunities rather than a series of borders. This project is a tribute to that transformation.
